headline: 25 May 2026
subtitle: US-Iran MOU not signed; wording disputes extend impasse; Brent falls below $100 on deal optimism

metrics.metric: Days since last confirmed cyber incident (Gulf FS)

items.text: No alerts reported in Kuwait in the past 24 hours. No air raid sirens, civil defense shelter-in-place orders, or emergency broadcasts were issued by Kuwait MOI or Kuwait Civil Defense during the coverage window. The next routine monthly siren test is scheduled for 1 June 2026 at 10:00 local time.
sources: US Embassy Kuwait advisory archive, 25 May 2026

items.text: No confirmed strikes, attacks, or military incidents targeting Kuwait or GCC territory reported in the past 24 hours. The April 8 ceasefire continues to hold for Day 47. US-Iran MOU negotiations are ongoing but no agreement was signed on Sunday 24 May as previously anticipated; US officials on Sunday actively tamped down expectations of an imminent announcement, citing remaining wording disputes. No new IRGC-affiliated incident reported in Kuwait or Gulf waters during the coverage window.

items.bold_heading: KWI: T1 phased resumption from 1 June remains on schedule; T4 and T5 fully operational.
items.text: GACA confirmed Terminal 1 will resume operations for Arab and international carriers from 1 June following completion of repair and rehabilitation works. Kuwait Airways (T4) and Jazeera Airways (T5) maintaining normal commercial schedules. Emirates running up to 5 daily flights. Qatar Airways restart targeted for 16 June (not yet confirmed). US State Dept Level 3 and UK FCDO advisories remain in effect for Kuwait.

items.bold_heading: Normal civil and workforce operations continue; no new closures.
items.text: Full civil government operations and secondary schools in-person since 3 May. No shelter-in-place orders, curfew, or emergency closures during coverage window.

items.bold_heading: Kuwait PM chairs FATF compliance meeting; AML enforcement escalating.
items.text: PM Sheikh Ahmad Al-Abdullah Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah chaired a high-level meeting in May to review FATF requirements following Kuwait's addition to the grey list in February 2026. The Ministry of Commerce issued Decision No. 25/2026 outlining an AML/CFT violation and penalty matrix with fines up to KD 500,000 (~USD 1.64M). KNBCC cybersecurity compliance Decision No. 2/2026 implementation window also remains open.

items.bold_heading: Shuwaikh Port: Security Level 2 maintained; daylight-only operations (05:00–19:00 UTC+3).
items.text: QR code (Sahel app) required for all maritime activity. Kuwait Chamber of Commerce coordination with King Abdullah Port and Fujairah bypass alternatives continues.

items.bold_heading: Land border crossings open with caveats.
items.text: Al-Nuwaisib (Saudi Arabia) and Abdali (Iraq/Safwan) crossings operational. Iraq-Kuwait border may close on short notice given residual IRGC-aligned militia threat in southern Iraq.

items.bold_heading: IRGC infiltrators trial continuing; no new security incident.
items.text: Four IRGC-linked operatives detained on Bubiyan Island on 1 May remain in criminal proceedings before Kuwait courts. No new incidents reported in coverage window.
No reported panic buying, essential supply disruptions, or curfew in coverage window.

items.bold_heading: US-Iran MOU not signed Sunday; wording disputes persist on Hormuz framing and sanctions timing.
items.text: Iran-US negotiators agreed on broad principles, but US officials on Sunday 24 May tamped down expectations, citing remaining back-and-forth on specific wording. Key sticking points: Hormuz 'management' framing; timing of sanctions waivers; and Iran's enriched uranium stockpile disposition. Trump said 'time is on our side' and instructed envoys not to rush.

items.bold_heading: Ceasefire holds (Day 47) but neither blockade has been lifted.
items.text: The US counter-blockade on Iranian ports (active since 13 April) and Iran's Strait of Hormuz constraints remain in parallel. A deal or breakdown will be the defining event of the coming week.

items.bold_heading: Israel-Hezbollah Lebanon clause remains a potential deal-breaker.
items.text: Netanyahu continues signaling Israel will not accept a deal clause constraining IDF operations against Hezbollah in Lebanon; Israel has continued strikes since the April 16 Lebanon ceasefire, killing 500+. A formal Israeli rejection could give Iran grounds to withdraw from the MOU.

items.bold_heading: Oil price drop on deal progress is real but not final; whipsaw risk is high this week.
items.text: Brent fell below $100 intraday Monday on Iran deal optimism. If the MOU is finalised this week, a further Brent move toward $80–$90 is plausible. However, if talks collapse, Brent could rebound sharply toward $110+. Dual-scenario margin and collateral protocols must remain active this week.

items.bold_heading: OFAC/sanctions: No waivers issued; compliance controls unchanged.
items.text: No MOU signature means no OFAC sanctions waivers. Institutions must not pre-emptively modify KYC/compliance controls for Iranian- or PGSA-adjacent counterparties until OFAC officially acts.

items.bold_heading: War risk premium repositioning premature; JWC JWLA-033 in force.
items.text: Marine WRP (~3–8% of vessel value vs. 0.1–0.25% pre-crisis) will not normalise on deal rumours alone. JWC requires sustained evidence of safe transit before any delisting. WRP assumptions should remain unchanged until JWC formally acts.

items.bold_heading: Kuwait FATF grey list compliance deadline pressure rising.
items.text: Decision No. 25/2026 penalty matrix now in force with fines up to KD 500,000. International correspondent banks applying enhanced due diligence to Kuwait-domiciled institutions. FATF remediation plans should align with PM-level compliance guidance issued this month.

items.bold_heading: Pro-Iran hacktivist tempo during deal-wording window: elevated.
items.text: Sunday deal failure may embolden IRGC-adjacent hardline hacktivist groups. SocRadar confirms 144+ verified GCC incidents since 28 Feb; financial services is the #2 most targeted sector. Monday-open window is historically high-activity; ensure SOC coverage is active.

items.bold_heading: JWC JWLA-033 remains in force; no delisting expected until verified, sustained Hormuz transit evidence accumulates.
items.text: With the MOU unsigned, the JWC has no basis to act. War risk premiums for Persian Gulf transits are at 3–8% of vessel value (vs. 0.1–0.25% pre-crisis), translating to USD 3–8M per VLCC voyage. Even on MOU signature, mine-clearance verification is required before underwriters normalise terms — a multi-week minimum runway.

items.bold_heading: Hormuz mine risk and transit cost remain structurally elevated.
items.text: JMIC Advisory Note 004-26 confirms mine risk near the Traffic Separation Scheme has not been fully surveyed. Current transit cost (~$2.5M VLCC voyage premium) will not snap back overnight even if the MOU is signed. Institutions financing tanker voyages should maintain elevated WRP assumptions for at least 4–6 weeks post-signature.

items.bold_heading: Hormuz Safe sanctions voidance risk persists.
items.text: Iran's PGSA-affiliated 'Hormuz Safe' insurance platform remains operational and non-compliant with OFAC sanctions. Acceptance of Hormuz Safe cover continues to risk automatic voidance of P&I Club and H&M cover. No OFAC exemption has been issued.

articles.title: Iran War Widens Gulf State Fissures
articles.author: Gaby Tejeda
articles.summary: Analyses how Iran's wartime strategy of targeting Gulf state infrastructure has widened pre-existing intra-Gulf rifts rather than unifying the GCC. The UAE has aligned decisively with the US and Israel; Saudi Arabia and Kuwait continue calling for de-escalation and diplomacy. Kuwait's oil exports face maximum Hormuz exposure, with no bypass pipeline alternatives available.
articles.relevance: Directly contextualises Kuwait's structural vulnerability in ongoing deal negotiations and explains why Kuwait's government stance (supporting de-escalation) aligns with its economic survival imperatives — critical backdrop for BCP and risk posture planning.
articles.note: Article dated 14 May 2026 — beyond 48h window; included for direct analytical relevance to Kuwait's strategic position. No qualifying 24–48h Tier 3 piece identified.

articles.title: The Insurance Weapon: How Commercial Risk Logic Became an Irregular Warfare Tool at Hormuz
articles.author: Irregular Warfare Center
articles.summary: Argues that commercial war-risk insurance has become a de facto instrument of economic coercion — Iran's ability to render shipping commercially uninsurable has achieved strategic effects equivalent to a kinetic blockade. Provides a framework for understanding why WRP will normalise slowly even after a deal: underwriter caution is structural, not just reactive.
articles.relevance: Essential analytical frame for Section 5A — explains why institutions must not expect rapid WRP normalisation post-MOU and why JWC delisting will lag market repricing.
articles.note: Article dated 13 May 2026 — beyond 48h window; included for direct relevance to Sections 5 and 5A. No qualifying 24–48h Tier 3 piece identified.

items.bold_heading: Pro-Iran hacktivist elevated posture: deal-wording impasse extends high-risk cyber window.
items.text: Pro-Iran hacktivist coalitions (313 Team, DieNet, Fatimion, ALTOUFAN TEAM) remain active. Sunday's failure to announce a signed MOU means hardline IRGC-adjacent groups retain a mobilisation rationale. SocRadar confirms 144+ verified GCC incidents since 28 Feb 2026; financial services is the #2 most targeted sector; Monday market open is a historically active attack window.
items.threat_actor: 313 Team; DieNet; Fatimion; ALTOUFAN TEAM (IRGC-adjacent hacktivist coalitions)
items.recommended_action: Maintain DDoS scrubbing, WAF, and CDN at full defensive posture; confirm SOC on-call coverage is active for Monday opening.

items.bold_heading: FINRA cybersecurity alert (active): elevated threat from Iranian cyber actors to financial sector.
items.text: FINRA's March 2026 advisory remains active, citing Iranian state-sponsored and Iran-aligned actors targeting US and GCC financial institutions with credential theft, ransomware, and DDoS. SISA InfoSec advisory identifies SWIFT messaging credential theft, payment gateway web shells, and Chaos ransomware false-flag attacks as live vectors.
items.recommended_action: Verify SWIFT CSP controls are current; review payment gateway access logs for anomalous after-hours activity ahead of Monday open.

items.bold_heading: APT34 (OilRig/IRGC) and MuddyWater: deal-wording delay preserves elevated cyber operational tempo.
items.text: A prolonged MOU impasse means IRGC cyber units will not stand down. APT34 TTPs remain active: StealHook Exchange credential exfiltration, DNS-tunnelling C2, Dindoor Deno backdoor, RustyWater RAT, Chaos ransomware false-flag. Dindoor backdoor has been observed in US banking sector compromises.
items.assessed_likelihood: Moderate (escalates to High if MOU collapses)

items.bold_heading: Chinese state-affiliated pre-positioning in Gulf financial and energy infrastructure: persistent.
items.text: CISA AA26-113a documents ongoing Chinese pre-positioning in GCC financial, communications, and energy infrastructure. No bilateral cyber stand-down agreement emerged from the Trump-Xi summit (14–15 May). Iran deal uncertainty may reduce China's immediate interest in active disruption but reconnaissance posture is assessed as sustained.

items.bold_heading: CISA AA26-097a: Iranian-affiliated PLC/OT exploitation posture unchanged.
items.text: Iran-affiliated actors continue targeting Rockwell Automation/Allen-Bradley PLCs; financial sector exposure via facility management, data-centre energy controls, and BMS systems remains. IRGC OT capabilities do not de-escalate automatically with a deal.
items.assessed_likelihood: Low-Moderate for direct Kuwait institutional OT targeting; High for continued global IRGC-affiliated OT campaign through 60-day MOU window if deal signed

items.text: Iran-US MOU wording resolution or breakdown.
items.why_it_matters: A signed MOU triggers Brent drop, WRP repricing, and Hormuz normalisation timeline; a breakdown could re-escalate kinetically and reverse Monday's oil price decline sharply.

items.text: Monday oil market close: deal pricing or reversal.
items.why_it_matters: If Brent closes below $95 on confirmed deal momentum, initiate active collateral and margin monitoring; if above $105 on collapse scenario, activate energy exposure escalation protocols.

items.text: Israel formal response on Lebanon clause.
items.why_it_matters: A formal Israeli rejection or continued IDF strikes in Lebanon at scale could provide Iran grounds to withdraw from the MOU, re-opening the path to kinetic escalation.

source_confidence.note: No alerts confirmed across KUNA, US Embassy archive, Kuwait MOI
Ceasefire hold confirmed across multiple Tier 1 wires; MOU not signed confirmed by CBS/Axios
KWI status, FATF, port/border all corroborated across multiple Tier 1 sources
Deal status confirmed by Axios, CBS, CNN with multiple official sources; Iran divergence noted
JWC/WRP figures from Albany Antree (14 May 2026); mine-risk via JMIC; confirmed unchanged
No new Kuwait-specific incident; posture inferred from deal-wording impasse and established threat intelligence
No new confirmed intrusion; likelihood assessments based on established actor TTPs and deal outcome scenarios
